FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study data (Data Release 4.0). Study explicitly states 'This study used data from the ABCD study (Data Release 4.0), an ongoing longitudinal study beginning in 2016 across 21 research sites in the US.' N=8979 with complete data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time variables: video streaming, online chatting (combined texting and video chatting), TV watching, and social media. All are platform-specific and qualify as smartphone/social media variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports temporal network analysis using Graphical Vector Autoregressive Model (GVAR) with multivariate associations between screen time variables and irritability. States 'temporal network, estimated by Graphical Vector Autoregressive Model, revealed bidirectional associations between irritability online chatting' with t-tests and partial correlations reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports numeric results including edge weights, p-values, and confidence intervals for network associations. Results section indicates 'irritability was negatively associated with time spent on video streaming at Year 2' and provides network statistics.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal study with three waves (baseline, Year 1, Year 2). Analyzes bidirectional temporal associations using novel statistical methods (GVAR).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology (2026). Full peer-reviewed publication with DOI 10.1177/10445463251415497.

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply because: (1) Paper reports bidirectional associations where screen time is BOTH predictor and outcome; (2) Irritability is a psychological/behavioral variable (not just demographic), making it an individual-characteristic outcome; (3) Paper includes both cross-sectional and temporal analyses; (4) Non-demographic predictor involved (emotion dysregulation).
notes: Clear inclusion. Reports bidirectional temporal associations between screen time variables and irritability as outcome using novel network methodology. Results controlled for ADHD, anxiety, and depression.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study data. Abstract explicitly states 'We used baseline and 2-year follow-up data (n = 7043) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study.' Methods confirm use of ABCD longitudinal data.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time variables: passive watching (TV/streaming), gaming, social media, and social engagement (text messaging, video chat on devices). These are platform-specific smartphone/social media variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports linear mixed-effect models examining associations between screen time variables and cognitive development outcomes. Methods state 'Linear mixed-effect models were employed to assess the association of modifiable lifestyle factors with the development of different cognitive functions over time' with t-values and p-values reported in tables.

FT_IC4.evidence: Provides numeric results in tables including β coefficients, standard errors, t-values, and p-values (e.g., Table 3: Passive watching B=-0.22, SE=0.05, t=-4.72, p<0.001 for inhibitory control).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal study with baseline and 2-year follow-up. Reports associations between modifiable lifestyle factors and cognitive development trajectories over time.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescence (2026, Open Access). Full peer-reviewed research article with DOI 10.1002/jad.70069.

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply because screen time is the INDEPENDENT variable predicting cognitive development outcomes (non-SM outcomes). Paper examines screen time as predictor of inhibitory control, information processing speed, visuospatial sequencing/memory, and verbal intellect.
notes: Clear inclusion. Reports screen time (including social media and social engagement) as predictor of multiple cognitive development outcomes. Sex moderation examined. Longitudinal design with extractable coefficients.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study data. Methods state 'we utilized the extensive adolescent brain cognitive development data set, which encompasses a wide range of environmental, social, and nutritional factors' and 'We used data from the National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) Data Archive Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) collection release 4.0.'

FT_IC2.evidence: Includes screen time as variable in exploratory factor analysis and regression models. Factor 5 identified as 'Screen time exposure (weekday; weekend)' - screen time measured as self-reported weekday and weekend usage qualifying as specific smartphone/SM variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports regression models analyzing relationships between risk groups (incorporating screen time) and behavioral/neural outcomes. Methods state 'Regression models were used to analyze the relationship between identified risk groups, and differences in reward sensitivity, and behavioral problems at 2-year follow-up.'

FT_IC4.evidence: Provides numeric results throughout including β values, confidence intervals, and p-values. For example, Figure 1 shows significant differences between risk groups in BAS drive and reward-related measures with reported statistics.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis combining exploratory factor analysis and regression modeling. Analyzes relationships between identified risk factors (including screen time) and behavioral/imaging outcomes at 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2025). Full peer-reviewed publication with DOI 10.1016/j.jadohealth.2023.08.033.

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply because: (1) Screen time is predictor variable in regression models predicting behavioral problems and neural outcomes (reward sensitivity, brain activation patterns); (2) Outcomes include behavioral and neural/imaging measures, not just demographic characteristics; (3) Non-demographic outcomes involved (reward sensitivity, behavioral problem traits).
notes: Inclusion. Screen time as one component of exploratory factor analysis predicting risk groups. Risk groups then predict behavioral and neural outcomes at follow-up. Screen time is measurable predictor with separate coefficient in composite variable.
